title: Swap nodes in pairs
description: Swap nodes in pairs recursively in linked list

examples.explanation: Step 1: Initialize dummy node (dummy.next = head=1). prev = dummy, first = 1, second = 2. Step 2: Swap pair (1,2): prev.next = 2, first.next = 3, second.next = 1. List becomes dummy->2->1->3->4. Update prev = 1. Step 3: Move to next pair: first = 3, second = 4. Swap: prev.next = 4, first.next = null, second.next = 3. List becomes dummy->2->1->4->3. Update prev = 3. Step 4: No more pairs, return dummy.next = 2.

Step 1: dummy node, prev = dummy, first = 1, second = 2. Step 2: Swap (1,2): prev.next = 2, first.next = 3, second.next = 1. List: dummy->2->1->3. prev = 1. Step 3: Next pair: first = 3, second = null (since only one node left). Stop. Return dummy.next = 2.

approaches.name: Brute Force Iterative O(n)
approaches.summary: Iterate through the linked list, swapping each pair of nodes by adjusting pointers directly. Use a dummy node to handle the head and maintain previous pointers for reconnection.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Swap Nodes in Pairs Iteratively
ladders.desc: Complete iterative solution to swap every two adjacent nodes in a linked list.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires swapping every two adjacent nodes in a linked list. The brute force iterative approach works by traversing the list one pair at a time, swapping the two nodes in each pair, and reconnecting them properly with the previous pair. We use a dummy node at the beginning to simplify edge cases, especially when swapping the head. The intuition is that we can treat each pair as a mini-reversal operation: we need to adjust pointers so that the second node points to the first, and the first points to the next pair. We maintain three pointers: prev (points to the node before the current pair), first (first node of the pair), and second (second node of the pair). In each iteration, we update prev.next to point to second, first.next to point to second.next, and second.next to point to first. Then we move prev to first (which is now the second node after swapping) and continue. The dummy node ensures we have a consistent prev even for the first pair. Time complexity is O(n) as we traverse each node once. Space complexity is O(1) as we use only a few pointers. This connects to L1 by implementing the full traversal and swap logic. Common mistakes include not handling odd-length lists correctly (where the last node remains unchanged), forgetting to update prev properly, or losing references during pointer adjustments. Always verify with edge cases like empty list, single node, or two nodes only.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize dummy node (dummy.next = head=1). prev = dummy, first = 1, second = 2. Step 2: Swap pair (1,2): prev.next = 2, first.next = 3, second.next = 1. List becomes dummy->2->1->3->4. Update prev = 1. Step 3: Move to next pair: first = 3, second = 4. Swap: prev.next = 4, first.next = null, second.next = 3. List becomes dummy->2->1->4->3. Update prev = 3. Step 4: No more pairs, return dummy.next = 2.

Step 1: dummy node, prev = dummy, first = 1, second = 2. Step 2: Swap (1,2): prev.next = 2, first.next = 3, second.next = 1. List: dummy->2->1->3. prev = 1. Step 3: Next pair: first = 3, second = null (since only one node left). Stop. Return dummy.next = 2.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Swap a Single Pair of Nodes
ladders.desc: Swap two adjacent nodes in a linked list given pointers to the previous node and the pair.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine focuses on swapping exactly two adjacent nodes (first and second) in a linked list, given a pointer to the node before them (prev). The intuition is that we need to rearrange pointers so that the second node comes before the first, while maintaining connections to the rest of the list. The algorithm: 1. Store first.next as next_node (the node after the pair). 2. Update prev.next to point to second. 3. Update first.next to point to next_node. 4. Update second.next to point to first. This effectively swaps the positions of first and second. Time complexity is O(1) per swap, as it involves constant pointer operations. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into L0 by being the core operation repeated for each pair in the full problem. Common mistakes include losing reference to next_node before updating pointers, or not updating prev.next correctly, which can break the list. Always ensure prev is not null and first and second are valid nodes. This sub-problem assumes the pair exists and is valid.

examples.trace: Step 1: next_node = first.next = 3. Step 2: prev.next = second (2). Step 3: first.next = next_node (3). Step 4: second.next = first (1). Result: prev points to 2, 2 points to 1, 1 points to 3.
Step 1: next_node = a.next = c. Step 2: prev.next = b. Step 3: a.next = c. Step 4: b.next = a. Result: prev->b->a->c.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Traversing and Identifying Pairs in Linked List
ladders.desc: Iterate through a linked list to identify and process adjacent pairs of nodes.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the logic to traverse a linked list and correctly identify each pair of adjacent nodes for swapping. The intuition is that we need to move through the list in steps of two nodes, ensuring we have both nodes in a pair before performing any operation. We use a while loop that continues as long as we have at least two nodes ahead. The algorithm: 1. Start with a pointer (e.g., current) at the head or a dummy node. 2. In each iteration, check if current.next and current.next.next exist (i.e., we have a pair). 3. If yes, extract first = current.next and second = current.next.next. 4. After processing the pair (e.g., swapping), update current to move past the swapped pair (e.g., current = first, since first becomes the second node after swap). 5. If not, break out of the loop. Time complexity is O(n) for traversal. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L1 by providing the framework to call the swap sub-routine for each pair. Common mistakes include not checking for null pointers before accessing next, which can cause errors, or incorrectly updating the current pointer, leading to infinite loops or missed nodes. Always validate that both nodes in the pair are non-null.

examples.trace: Step 1: Start with current = dummy (pointing to 1). Check: current.next=1 and current.next.next=2 exist. Pair (1,2). Step 2: After processing, update current = 1. Step 3: Check: current.next=3 and current.next.next=4 exist. Pair (3,4). Step 4: After processing, update current = 3. Step 5: Check: current.next=null, stop.

Step 1: current = dummy (pointing to a). Check: current.next=a and current.next.next=b exist. Pair (a,b). Step 2: After processing, update current = a. Step 3: Check: current.next=c, but current.next.next=null, so no full pair. Stop.

ladders.title: Building Block — Using a Dummy Node for Linked List Head Handling
ladders.desc: Create and use a dummy node to simplify operations at the head of a linked list.
ladders.explanation: A dummy node is an auxiliary node placed before the actual head of a linked list. Its purpose is to provide a consistent starting point for pointer manipulations, especially when the head might change (e.g., during swaps). The intuition is that by having a dummy node, we can treat all nodes uniformly, avoiding special cases for the head. The algorithm: 1. Create a dummy node with any value (e.g., 0). 2. Set dummy.next = head. 3. Use dummy as the initial previous pointer in traversal. 4. After processing, return dummy.next as the new head. Time and space complexity are O(1) for the dummy node itself. This connects to L2 by enabling safe traversal and pointer updates without null checks for the head. Common mistakes include forgetting to update dummy.next after swaps, or not returning dummy.next at the end, which can return the dummy node instead of the actual list. Always initialize dummy properly and ensure it points to the correct head after operations.

examples.input: List: 1->2->3, need to swap first pair
examples.trace: Step 1: Create dummy node (value=0). dummy.next = 1. Step 2: Use prev = dummy, first = 1, second = 2. Step 3: Swap: dummy.next = 2, 1.next = 3, 2.next = 1. Step 4: Return dummy.next = 2.

examples.output: Dummy node used, new head = null
examples.trace: Step 1: Create dummy node. dummy.next = null. Step 2: No operations. Step 3: Return dummy.next = null.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Pointer Manipulation in Linked List
ladders.desc: Change the next pointers of nodes to rearrange links in a linked list.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the fundamental operation of updating the 'next' pointers of nodes in a linked list to change the order of nodes. The intuition is that a linked list is defined by these pointers, so by reassigning them, we can modify the structure without moving data. The algorithm involves simple assignments: node1.next = node2 to link node1 to node2. To swap two nodes, we need to carefully update multiple pointers in the correct order to avoid losing references. For example, to swap A and B where prev->A->B->next, we do: prev.next = B, A.next = next, B.next = A. Time complexity is O(1) per pointer change. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L3 as the dummy node uses pointer manipulation to link to the head. Common mistakes include updating pointers in the wrong order (e.g., setting A.next before saving its original value), which can break the list, or not handling null pointers. Always use temporary variables if needed to store references before changing them.

examples.input: List: X->Y->Z, change X.next to Z
examples.trace: Step 1: Original: X.next = Y, Y.next = Z. Step 2: Execute X.next = Z. Now X points to Z, Y is no longer reachable from X.

examples.input: List: A->B->C, swap A and B with prev=null
examples.trace: Step 1: Store B.next = C. Step 2: Set B.next = A. Step 3: Set A.next = C. Result: B points to A, A points to C.

testCases.input: Node A, set A.next = B where B is null
Node C, set C.next = D where D.next = E
List: F->G, set F.next = H, G.next = I
List: K->L->M, set L.next = N, M.next = O

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Linked List Node Structure and Traversal
ladders.desc: Understand the basic structure of a linked list node and how to traverse from one node to the next.
ladders.explanation: A linked list is a linear data structure where each element (node) contains data and a pointer/reference to the next node. The foundation concept involves understanding this node structure: typically defined as a class with 'val' (data) and 'next' (pointer to next node). Traversal means starting from the head (first node) and moving sequentially by following the 'next' pointers until reaching null (end of list). The intuition is that the list is chained together by these pointers, allowing dynamic size and efficient insertions/deletions. Algorithm for traversal: 1. Start with current = head. 2. While current is not null: process current.val, then set current = current.next. Time complexity is O(n) for full traversal. Space complexity is O(1) if using iterative traversal. This connects to L4 as pointer manipulation builds on this structure. Common mistakes include infinite loops by not updating current, or null pointer errors by accessing current.next when current is null. Beginners should practice creating simple lists and traversing them to print values.

examples.trace: Step 1: current = 1, print 1, current = 1.next = 2. Step 2: current = 2, print 2, current = 2.next = 3. Step 3: current = 3, print 3, current = 3.next = null. Stop.
Step 1: current = a, print a, current = a.next = b. Step 2: current = b, print b, current = b.next = null. Stop.

approaches.name: Recursive O(n)
approaches.summary: Recursively swap pairs by handling the first two nodes and then recursively processing the rest of the list. Base case handles when fewer than two nodes remain.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Recursively Swap Nodes in Pairs
ladders.desc: Complete recursive solution to swap every two adjacent nodes in a linked list
ladders.explanation: This is the complete problem where we implement a recursive solution to swap nodes in pairs. The intuition is elegant: treat the linked list as pairs of nodes, swap the first two nodes, then recursively process the rest. The base case handles when we have 0 or 1 node left (nothing to swap). The recursive approach naturally follows the problem structure - each pair is independent, so we can solve for one pair and delegate the rest to recursion. The algorithm: 1) Base case: if head is null or head.next is null, return head (nothing to swap). 2) Store the second node as 'nextPair' (this will be the head of the next recursive call). 3) Make the second node point to the first node (swapping their positions). 4) Make the first node point to the result of recursively calling the function on 'nextPair'. 5) Return the new head (which was originally the second node). Time complexity is O(n) as we visit each node once. Space complexity is O(n) due to recursion stack depth. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete recursive swap function. Common mistakes include: forgetting base cases (causing infinite recursion), incorrectly updating pointers (especially losing reference to next nodes), and not handling edge cases like empty lists or single nodes. The recursive pattern elegantly mirrors the problem's natural structure.

examples.trace: Step 1: Call swapPairs(1->2->3->4). Head=1, head.next=2. Store nextPair=3->4. Make 2 point to 1 (2->1). Recursively call swapPairs(3->4). Step 2: In swapPairs(3->4), head=3, head.next=4. Store nextPair=null. Make 4 point to 3 (4->3). Recursively call swapPairs(null). Step 3: swapPairs(null) returns null. Make 3 point to null (3->null). Return 4 as new head (4->3->null). Step 4: Back to first call, make 1 point to result from recursion (4). So 1->4->3->null. Return 2 as new head. Final: 2->1->4->3->null.

Step 1: Call swapPairs(1->2->3). Head=1, head.next=2. Store nextPair=3. Make 2 point to 1 (2->1). Recursively call swapPairs(3). Step 2: In swapPairs(3), head=3, head.next=null (base case). Return 3 unchanged. Step 3: Back to first call, make 1 point to result from recursion (3). So 1->3. Return 2 as new head. Final: 2->1->3->null.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Pair Swapping Function
ladders.desc: Implement the core recursive function that swaps one pair and recurses on the rest
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the recursive function that forms the heart of the solution. The intuition is divide-and-conquer: handle the first pair locally, then delegate the remaining list to the same function. The function takes a head node and returns the new head after swapping all pairs. The algorithm: 1) Base case: if head is null or head.next is null, return head (no swap needed). 2) Identify the two nodes to swap: first = head, second = head.next. 3) Store the start of next pair: nextPair = second.next. 4) Perform the swap: make second point to first. 5) Recursively process the rest: first.next = recursiveCall(nextPair). 6) Return second as the new head. Time complexity for this function is O(n) as it processes each node once. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion stack. This connects to L0 as the complete implementation, and to L2 as it uses the core swapping logic. Common mistakes: not checking head.next before accessing it (null pointer error), losing reference to nextPair before recursion, and incorrect base case conditions. The recursive pattern is clean: swap current pair, attach result of recursive call, return new head.

examples.trace: Function call with head=A. Step 1: A not null, A.next=B not null. Step 2: first=A, second=B. Step 3: nextPair=C. Step 4: B.next=A (swap). Step 5: Recursive call with head=C. In recursion: C not null, C.next=D not null. first=C, second=D, nextPair=null. D.next=C. Recursive call with null returns null. C.next=null. Return D. Step 6: Back to first call: A.next=D. Return B. Final: B->A->D->C.

Function call with head=X. Step 1: X not null, X.next=Y not null. Step 2: first=X, second=Y. Step 3: nextPair=null. Step 4: Y.next=X. Step 5: Recursive call with null returns null. X.next=null. Step 6: Return Y. Final: Y->X->null.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Swapping Two Adjacent Nodes
ladders.desc: Swap two consecutive nodes in a linked list while maintaining connections
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the fundamental operation of swapping two adjacent nodes. The intuition is that we need to rearrange pointers so that the second node comes before the first, while preserving the connection to the rest of the list. The key insight is that we need three references: the first node, the second node, and what comes after them. The algorithm: 1) Given head pointing to first node. 2) If head or head.next is null, return (nothing to swap). 3) Let first = head, second = head.next. 4) Store nextPair = second.next (the node after the pair). 5) Perform swap: second.next = first (second now points to first). 6) first.next = nextPair (first now points to what was after second). 7) The new head is second. Time complexity is O(1) for a single swap. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L1 as the core operation within the recursive function, and to L3 which provides the node structure. Common mistakes: losing reference to nextPair before updating pointers, not updating the head reference after swap, and attempting to swap when nodes don't exist. The pointer manipulation must be done in correct order to avoid losing references.

examples.trace: Step 1: head points to Node1. Step 2: Node1 and Node2 exist. Step 3: first=Node1, second=Node2. Step 4: nextPair=Node3. Step 5: second.next=Node1 (Node2 now points to Node1). Step 6: first.next=Node3 (Node1 now points to Node3). Step 7: New head is Node2. Final: Node2->Node1->Node3.
Step 1: head points to A. Step 2: A and B exist. Step 3: first=A, second=B. Step 4: nextPair=C. Step 5: B.next=A. Step 6: A.next=C. Step 7: New head is B. Final: B->A->C->D.

ladders.title: Building Block — Linked List Node Structure and Traversal
ladders.desc: Understand and work with linked list nodes, pointers, and basic traversal
ladders.explanation: This level covers the fundamental building block of linked lists: nodes with data and next pointers. The intuition is that a linked list is a chain of nodes where each node points to the next, allowing dynamic size and efficient insertions/deletions. Each node typically has: data (value) and next (pointer/reference to next node). The last node's next is null. Traversal involves starting at head and following next pointers until null. The algorithm for traversal: 1) Start with current = head. 2) While current is not null: process current.data, then current = current.next. Time complexity for traversal is O(n). Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L2 as we need to understand nodes to swap them, and to L4 which covers basic pointer operations. Common mistakes: accessing null pointers, infinite loops by not advancing current, and misunderstanding that 'next' is a reference/pointer not the actual next node. Real code pattern: class Node with data and next attributes. Understanding this structure is crucial for manipulating pointers in swapping.

examples.trace: Step 1: head points to node with data=1, next=node2. Step 2: current=head (node1). Process data=1. current=node1.next (node2). Step 3: current=node2. Process data=2. current=node2.next (node3). Step 4: current=node3. Process data=3. current=node3.next (null). Step 5: current=null, stop.

Step 1: head points to nodeA. current=nodeA. Process A. current=nodeA.next (nodeB). Step 2: current=nodeB. Process B. current=nodeB.next (nodeC). Step 3: current=nodeC. Process C. current=nodeC.next (nodeD). Step 4: current=nodeD. Process D. current=nodeD.next (null). Step 5: Stop.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Pointer/Reference Manipulation
ladders.desc: Change what a pointer/reference points to in memory
ladders.explanation: This level covers the basic operation of changing pointer values, which is fundamental to linked list manipulations. The intuition is that pointers/references are variables that store memory addresses, and by changing these addresses, we can rearrange how nodes are connected. In linked lists, the 'next' pointer in a node determines which node comes after it. The key operations: 1) Assignment: node.next = otherNode (makes node point to otherNode). 2) Access: value = node.next (gets the node being pointed to). 3) Null assignment: node.next = null (breaks connection). The algorithm for swapping pointers between two nodes: temp = node1.next; node1.next = node2.next; node2.next = temp. Time and space complexity are O(1). This connects to L3 as we use pointer manipulation to traverse and modify linked lists, and to L5 which covers basic variables. Common mistakes: losing original pointers by overwriting them without saving, dereferencing null pointers, and confusing pointer assignment with value assignment. Understanding that 'node.next = otherNode' changes the connection, not copies data, is crucial.

examples.input: Node A points to B, Node B points to C. Make A point to C.
examples.trace: Step 1: Initial state: A.next = B, B.next = C. Step 2: Execute A.next = B.next. Step 3: B.next is C, so A.next becomes C. Step 4: Final: A points to C, B still points to C.

examples.input: Node X points to Y, Node Y points to null. Make Y point to X.
examples.trace: Step 1: Initial: X.next = Y, Y.next = null. Step 2: Execute Y.next = X. Step 3: Y.next becomes X. Step 4: Final: X points to Y, Y points to X (creating a cycle).

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Variables and Basic Data Types
ladders.desc: Understand variables, values, and basic data types like integers and references
ladders.explanation: This is the foundational concept for beginners: understanding variables as named storage locations that hold values. The intuition is that in programming, we use variables to store and manipulate data. A variable has a name, a type, and a value. Basic types include integers (whole numbers), strings (text), and references/pointers (memory addresses). For linked lists, we need to understand that a node variable stores a reference to a node object in memory. Key concepts: 1) Variable declaration: creating a variable with a name. 2) Assignment: giving a value to a variable using '='. 3) Access: using the variable name to get its value. 4) Null: a special value meaning 'no reference'. The algorithm for basic variable usage: declare variable, assign value, use variable in operations. Time and space are O(1). This connects to L4 as pointer manipulation builds on variable assignment. Common mistakes: confusing variable names with values, not initializing variables before use, and misunderstanding null. For example, 'node = otherNode' makes 'node' refer to the same object as 'otherNode', not a copy.

examples.input: Create variable x with value 5, then change it to 10
examples.trace: Step 1: Declare variable x. Step 2: Assign x = 5. Now x holds value 5. Step 3: Assign x = 10. Now x holds value 10. The old value 5 is overwritten.

examples.input: Create variable ptr that points to a node with data 100
examples.trace: Step 1: Create a node object in memory with data=100. Step 2: Declare variable ptr. Step 3: Assign ptr = reference to that node. Now ptr 'points to' or 'refers to' the node. We can access node data via ptr.data.

testCases.input: Set x=5, y=6, then swap: temp=x, x=y, y=temp
Set a=1, b=2, c=3, then a=c

approaches.name: Two Pointer with Dummy Node O(n)
approaches.summary: Use two pointers to track the current pair and a dummy node to simplify edge cases. Swap nodes by adjusting pointers in a single pass without recursion.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Swap Nodes in Pairs Using Two Pointer with Dummy Node
ladders.desc: Complete iterative solution to swap every two adjacent nodes in a linked list using a dummy node and two pointers.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the problem by iterating through the linked list and swapping adjacent nodes in pairs. The intuition is to use a dummy node at the beginning to handle edge cases like swapping the first two nodes, and two pointers (prev and curr) to track the nodes being swapped. The algorithm works by adjusting pointers in a single pass: for each pair, we temporarily store the second node, adjust the next pointers of the previous node and the two nodes in the pair, then move the pointers forward. This avoids recursion and uses constant extra space. The time complexity is O(n) as we traverse each node once, and space complexity is O(1) since we only use a few pointers. Connection to L1: This level implements the full solution by using the pointer adjustment logic from L1 within a loop. Common mistakes include not updating pointers correctly (leading to cycles or lost nodes) and not handling odd-length lists properly. Real code pattern: Initialize dummy node pointing to head, set prev = dummy, curr = head. While curr and curr.next exist: store second = curr.next, adjust prev.next to second, curr.next to second.next, second.next to curr, then move prev = curr and curr = curr.next. Return dummy.next.

examples.trace: Start: dummy->1->2->3->4, prev=dummy, curr=1. Pair 1-2: second=2, prev.next=2, curr.next=3, second.next=1, list: dummy->2->1->3->4. Update prev=1, curr=3. Pair 3-4: second=4, prev.next=4, curr.next=null, second.next=3, list: dummy->2->1->4->3. Update prev=3, curr=null. End: return dummy.next=2.

Start: dummy->1->2->3, prev=dummy, curr=1. Pair 1-2: second=2, prev.next=2, curr.next=3, second.next=1, list: dummy->2->1->3. Update prev=1, curr=3. No pair since curr.next is null. End: return dummy.next=2.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Swap a Single Pair of Adjacent Nodes
ladders.desc: Swap two consecutive nodes in a linked list given pointers to the node before the pair and the first node of the pair.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core operation for swapping a pair of nodes. The intuition is to rearrange the next pointers of three nodes: the node before the pair (prev), the first node (first), and the second node (second). The algorithm: given prev, first, and second, set prev.next = second, first.next = second.next, and second.next = first. This effectively inserts second before first and updates connections. Time complexity is O(1) as it involves constant pointer assignments, and space complexity is O(1). Connection to L0: This routine is called repeatedly in a loop in the full solution. Common mistakes include losing reference to the rest of the list by not updating first.next correctly or creating cycles. Real code pattern: def swap_pair(prev, first): second = first.next; prev.next = second; first.next = second.next; second.next = first.

examples.trace: Start: prev points to node before 1, first=1, second=2. Step 1: prev.next = 2. Step 2: first.next = 3. Step 3: second.next = 1. Result: prev->2->1->3.
Start: dummy points to node before 5, first=5, second=6. Step 1: dummy.next = 6. Step 2: 5.next = 7. Step 3: 6.next = 5. Result: dummy->6->5->7.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterating Through Pairs with Pointer Updates
ladders.desc: Traverse a linked list in steps of two nodes, updating pointers to move to the next pair after swapping.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the iteration logic that allows moving from one pair to the next after a swap. The intuition is to maintain two pointers: one to track the node before the current pair (prev) and one for the first node of the pair (curr). After swapping the pair, we update prev to the new first node (which was originally the second node) and curr to the node after the swapped pair. This ensures we correctly position for the next iteration. The algorithm: while curr and curr.next exist, perform the swap using prev and curr, then set prev = curr and curr = curr.next. Time complexity for this logic in isolation is O(n) if embedded in a loop, but here we analyze the pointer update step as O(1). Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L1: This logic uses the swap routine from L1 and then updates pointers to advance. Common mistakes include not updating prev correctly (leading to missed nodes) or updating curr before prev. Real code pattern: After swap_pair(prev, curr), set prev = curr; curr = curr.next.

examples.input: After swapping pair 1-2 in list dummy->1->2->3->4, with prev=dummy, curr=1
examples.trace: Start: prev=dummy, curr=1. After swap: list is dummy->2->1->3->4. Now, prev should point to node before next pair (which is node 1), so prev = curr = 1. curr should be the first of next pair (node 3), so curr = curr.next = 3.

examples.input: After swapping pair 3-4 in list dummy->2->1->3->4->5, with prev=1, curr=3
examples.trace: Start: prev=1, curr=3. After swap: list is dummy->2->1->4->3->5. Update prev = curr = 3. Update curr = curr.next = 5.

testCases.input: prev=A, curr=B after swap in A->B->C->D
prev=X, curr=Y after swap in X->Y->Z
prev=p, curr=q after swap in p->q->r
prev=a, curr=b after swap in a->b->c->d->e
prev=m, curr=n after swap in m->n->o->p
prev=x, curr=y after swap in x->y->z->w

ladders.title: Building Block — Using a Dummy Node for Edge Case Handling
ladders.desc: Create and utilize a dummy node at the start of a linked list to simplify operations on the head.
ladders.explanation: A dummy node is a common technique in linked list problems to avoid special cases when modifying the head. The intuition is to add a temporary node that points to the original head, so all operations can treat the list uniformly. This is crucial here because swapping the first pair changes the head of the list. The algorithm: create a dummy node with dummy.next = head, use dummy as the initial prev pointer, and after processing, return dummy.next as the new head. Time complexity is O(1) for setup, and space complexity is O(1) for the extra node. Connection to L2: This dummy node serves as the initial prev in the iteration logic. Common mistakes include not returning dummy.next or modifying dummy incorrectly. Real code pattern: dummy = ListNode(0); dummy.next = head; prev = dummy; ...; return dummy.next.

examples.trace: Create a new node dummy with value 0. Set dummy.next = head (node 1). Now the list is dummy->1->2->3. Use dummy as prev for first swap.
Create dummy node. Set dummy.next = null (since head is null). List is dummy->null. Return dummy.next which is null.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Traversing a Linked List with a While Loop
ladders.desc: Iterate through each node in a linked list using a pointer and a while loop.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves moving through a linked list node by node. The intuition is to start at the head and use a current pointer that advances to the next node until reaching the end (null). This is essential for accessing nodes to swap. The algorithm: initialize curr = head; while curr is not null: process curr, then set curr = curr.next. Time complexity is O(n) for traversing n nodes, and space complexity is O(1). Connection to L3: This traversal is used within the loop that applies the dummy node technique. Common mistakes include infinite loops by not updating curr or losing the head reference. Real code pattern: curr = head; while curr: # do something; curr = curr.next.

examples.trace: Start curr=1. While curr not null: visit curr=1, update curr=2. Visit curr=2, update curr=3. Visit curr=3, update curr=null. End.
Start curr=10. Visit curr=10, update curr=20. Visit curr=20, update curr=null. End.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Linked List Node Structure and Pointers
ladders.desc: Understand the basic structure of a linked list node and how pointers connect nodes.
ladders.explanation: A linked list is a data structure where each node contains data and a pointer (or reference) to the next node. The intuition is that nodes are linked sequentially, allowing dynamic size and efficient insertions/deletions. This foundation is crucial because swapping nodes involves manipulating these pointers. The algorithm: define a node class with attributes data and next. For example, in Python: class ListNode: def __init__(self, val=0, next=None): self.val = val; self.next = next. Time and space complexity are not applicable here as it's a conceptual foundation. Connection to L4: This structure is what we traverse in the basic operation. Common mistakes include confusing nodes with their values or not understanding that next is a pointer to another node. Real code pattern: Creating nodes: node1 = ListNode(1); node2 = ListNode(2); node1.next = node2 forms 1->2.

examples.trace: Step 1: Create node1 with val=1, next=None. Step 2: Create node2 with val=2, next=None. Step 3: Set node1.next = node2. Now node1 points to node2, forming 1->2.

examples.input: Create a single node with value 5
examples.trace: Create node with val=5, next=None. This is a list with one node: 5.
